narrative: As a white male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and an LSAT score of 35, your academic profile shows solid but not standout performance compared to peers, and your upper-middle family income places you in a stable bracket. The university's admission model, trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try, estimated a 61.2% chance that you would fail the bar—above the threshold they use for approval, leading to your application's rejection. This prediction hinges on a few key aspects of your record that carried the most weight in the decision. The strongest influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which is notably lower than the typical 5th or 6th decile for others, signaling weaker performance in later undergraduate years when the model sees bar success as more challenging. Next, your LSAT score of 35 worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing since it's close to the average of 36.7 and shows competitive reasoning ability. Your Year 1 decile of 2nd also nudged things toward failure, again below the usual 5th or 6th decile average, highlighting an early pattern of grades that the model links to bar struggles. Full-time enrollment plans and your background aligned with common profiles, like the 92.7% of students in similar situations, so they didn't sway the outcome much. Overall, your later-year grades stood out as the biggest red flag compared to typical admitted students, who tend to climb into higher deciles by Year 3. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above average or strengthening your final-year performance through targeted courses or tutoring, then reapply with that evidence of growth. Many students in your position have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps, so reviewing your transcript for improvement strategies could make a real difference next time.
